Influenza in Madagascar and Africa

GISAID EpiFlu Metadata Analysis

Author

Camila Duitama

Published

April 23, 2026

1 Overview

This report summarises all influenza sequences available in GISAID EpiFlu for Africa (all hosts, all types) with a focus on Madagascar. Data were downloaded on 16 March 2026.

A sample is considered analysis-ready if it meets all three criteria:

  1. Complete genome — all 8 segments (PB2, PB1, PA, HA, NP, NA, MP, NS) present in the downloaded FASTA and each segment above minimum length thresholds (PB2/PB1 ≥ 1,800 nt, PA ≥ 1,700, HA ≥ 1,300, NP ≥ 1,200, NA ≥ 1,000, MP ≥ 800, NS ≥ 700)
  2. Known geographic location — Country field non-empty
  3. Known sampling date — Collection date present and plausible (1990–2026). All isolates in this dataset have full year-month-day precision (0 are year-only or month-only).

The two GISAID downloads are merged and deduplicated on Isolate_Id, with the most specific download taking precedence.

2.6 Influenza B lineage (Africa)

Among the 5081 Influenza B isolates from Africa, the Victoria lineage predominates. A small fraction have no lineage annotation in GISAID.

3.4 Influenza B lineage (Madagascar)

All 631 Influenza B isolates from Madagascar have a lineage annotation. The Victoria lineage accounts for the vast majority.

3.5 Analysis-ready isolates

3.6 Analysis-ready isolates by subtype (top 6)

3.7 Norosoa — Unpublished Avian Sequences

Unpublished avian influenza sequences collected by Norosoa Razanajatovo (Institut Pasteur de Madagascar, 2021–2023). These 109 isolates (830 segment sequences) are not publicly available in GISAID (except 3 partially-released H9N2 isolates) and represent the only near-complete avian influenza genomic data from Madagascar in this analysis.

Data quality notes:

  • 3 isolates (78609-23, 78830-23, 79149-23 — H9N2, July 2023) exist in GISAID with only 5 segments. The Norosoa FASTAs contain CDC-sequenced complete genomes and supersede those entries in the alignments.
  • 20 isolates are partially typed (N2 and N6 files): only the NA segment was sequenced; HA subtype is unknown. Included in NA and internal-segment trees only.
  • 1 confirmed co-infection (H1N2/H6N1): sequences retained under the dominant subtype.
  • 1 imported case (80824-23, Chicken, H4N6, 22 Aug 2023, region “Brésil”): day-old chick imported from Brazil — excluded from African diversity assessments.

3.7.1 Subtype distribution

3.7.2 Sampling timeline

3.7.3 Genome completeness

Each isolate may have been sequenced for some or all 8 gene segments (PB2, PB1, PA, HA, NP, NA, MP, NS). Completeness is estimated from the number of sequences per isolate in the FASTA files (≥ 7 sequences ≈ complete genome).

4 Sequence Quality

For each isolate with sequences, we computed the length of every segment available in the FASTA files. This section shows whether segments are long enough for phylogenetic analysis.

4.1 Segment length distribution (Africa, complete genomes)

4.2 Phylo-ready isolates per country (top 10)

5 Phylogenetic Analysis

10 maximum-likelihood trees (IQ-TREE 3.1.0, HKY+G, UFBoot2 1000 replicates) were built for HA and NA segments across 5 subtypes (B, H1N1, H3N2, H6N2, H9N2). Monophyletic Madagascar clades were detected with scripts/analyse_trees.py (ete3, midpoint-rooted, maximal all-Madagascar subtrees ≥ 2 tips).

→ Full phylogenetic analysis, tree visualizations and clade statistics

6 Data Sources

  • GISAID EpiFlu (https://gisaid.org) — data downloaded on 16 March 2026. Madagascar dataset: all hosts, all influenza types. Africa dataset: all hosts, all African countries, all influenza types.

  • Norosoa Razanajatovo (Institut Pasteur de Madagascar) — unpublished avian influenza sequences from Madagascar, collected 2021–2023, provided directly to this analysis. 109 unique isolates, 830 segment sequences, covering subtypes H1N2, H4, H6N2, H6N8, H7N7, H9N2 and partial-subtype isolates (N2, N6). Complete genomes assembled from CDC sequencing. Not yet publicly available in GISAID (3 partial H9N2 entries exist: 78609-23, 78830-23, 79149-23 — superseded by these complete genomes in this analysis).
